linear.note: Measured along centreline for pipes and ducts. Cable measured along containment route + drops.

area.note: Rectangular duct: 2(W+H) × Length = m². Equipment: measure actual surface area.

number.note: Count individually. Equipment must include full specification.

weight.note: Only used where M&E subcontractor provides structural supports. Rare in standard M&E takeoff.

item.note: Used for non-quantifiable scope items. Always describe what the item covers.

units.rule: NEVER mix units within the same line item. Each line item has exactly ONE unit. If an item needs multiple units (e.g. pipe + insulation), they are SEPARATE line items.

copper.reason: Offcuts from standard 3m or 6m lengths, bending waste, joint preparation
copper.notes: Use 10% for long straight runs, 15% for complex plant rooms with many short sections

carbon_steel_screwed.reason: Threading waste, cutting allowance, nipple offcuts
carbon_steel_screwed.notes: Higher waste than welded due to threading requirements at each joint

carbon_steel_welded.reason: Cutting waste, weld preparation, alignment adjustments
carbon_steel_welded.notes: Lower waste — pipes cut to exact length and welded. Standard 6m lengths.

cpvc_upvc.reason: Solvent weld preparation, brittle material, cutting waste
cpvc_upvc.notes: Slightly higher waste than copper due to brittleness

mdpe.reason: Coil end waste, fusion joint preparation
mdpe.notes: Supplied in coils (25m, 50m, 100m) — less waste than rigid pipe

stainless_steel.reason: Precision cutting, press-fit preparation, material cost makes waste significant
stainless_steel.notes: Higher material cost — waste factor is commercially important

mlcp.reason: Coil supply, press-fit jointing, minimal cutting waste
mlcp.notes: Multi-layer composite pipe. Supplied in coils. Low waste.

general_wiring.reason: Drum end waste, routing adjustments, stripping waste
general_wiring.notes: Standard allowance for LSZH, SWA, twin & earth cable runs

containment_runs.reason: Cutting waste at junctions, offcuts from standard 3m lengths
containment_runs.notes: Cable tray, trunking, conduit — lower waste than cable due to rigid routing

fire_performance.reason: FP200/MICC is harder to work with, more waste at terminations
fire_performance.notes: Fire performance cable is stiffer and requires more care in bending

data_cable.reason: Service loops at patch panels, testing loops, re-termination allowance
data_cable.notes: Higher waste — data cables need service loops and may require re-termination

termination_allowance.reason: Extra cable at each end for stripping, connection, and service loop
termination_allowance.notes: Add this PER TERMINATION POINT on top of the measured route length

rectangular.reason: Sheet metal offcuts, joint allowances, seam waste, flange material
rectangular.notes: Applied to the calculated surface area: 2(W+H) × L × (1 + waste%)

circular.reason: Pre-formed spiral duct has less waste than fabricated rectangular
circular.notes: Spiral duct supplied in standard lengths (3m typically). Less site fabrication.

flat_oval.notes: Flat oval used where height is restricted but round duct performance desired

flexible.reason: Routing adjustments, compression, offcuts, connection waste
flexible.notes: Flex duct has highest waste — it compresses during installation and routing often changes on site

pipe_sections.reason: Valve boxing, flange covers, fitting insulation, joints, offcuts at bends
pipe_sections.notes: Includes allowance for insulating around valves, flanges, and fittings which use more material per metre

duct_wrap.reason: Overlaps at joints, wastage at corners and offsets, pins and tape
duct_wrap.notes: Duct wrap is sheet material — overlaps at joints typically 50-75mm each side

duct_board.reason: Cutting waste from standard sheet sizes, notching at corners
duct_board.notes: Rigid board insulation cut from standard 1200×600 or 1200×1000 sheets

equipment.reason: Bespoke fabrication, cutting from sheets, curved surface waste
equipment.notes: Equipment insulation is often bespoke — jackets or measured/cut on site

application_rules: Waste factors are applied AFTER net measurement — they are a MULTIPLIER on the measured quantity.
Formula: Gross Qty = Net Qty × (1 + Waste Factor). Example: 100m pipe × 1.12 = 112m to order.
Always show waste as a SEPARATE line or note — never hide it in the measured quantity.
Estimator can adjust waste factors based on project-specific knowledge.
Use LOW end for simple, straight routing. Use HIGH end for complex plant rooms.
For NRM2 BoQ: quantities are NET (no waste). Waste is included in the RATE.
For material ordering: quantities INCLUDE waste.

fittings_allowance.when_to_estimate: Only estimate fittings when individual fittings are NOT shown on drawings AND no fittings schedule exists.
fittings_allowance.when_NOT_to_estimate: If a fittings schedule exists in the specification or on a drawing, use the schedule — NEVER estimate when a schedule is available.
priority_order: 1. Use fittings schedule (from spec or drawings) — MOST ACCURATE
2. Count individually from GA drawings — ACCURATE
3. Estimate using allowance rates below — LEAST ACCURATE, flag as estimated

copper_small.notes: Small bore domestic/commercial runs change direction frequently

copper_medium.notes: Medium bore — fewer direction changes than small bore

copper_large.notes: Large bore copper — longer straight runs, fewer bends

steel_medium.notes: Medium bore steel — main distribution runs

steel_large.notes: Large bore steel — main risers and headers

riser_additional: 2× elbows at base of riser (horizontal to vertical)
2× elbows at top of riser (vertical to horizontal)
1× isolation valve per floor (if spec requires)

ductwork_fittings.rule: Ductwork fittings should ALWAYS be counted individually from GA drawings.
ductwork_fittings.if_not_shown: If ductwork fittings are not individually shown, allow 25% of straight duct quantity as fittings cost (Wendes method). Flag as estimated.

small_bore.type: Pipe clips, munsen rings, or channel brackets
small_bore.notes: BS 5422 / BSRIA guidance. Closer spacing for horizontal runs. Vertical runs need guide brackets.

medium_bore.type: Pipe brackets, roller supports, channel with beam clamps
medium_bore.notes: Heavier fixings required. Check structural capacity of ceiling/steelwork.

large_bore.type: Trapeze hangers, clevis hangers, roller supports
large_bore.notes: May need structural engineer sign-off for support point loads.

riser_supports.notes: Add 20% to horizontal support quantities for riser sections. Risers need guide brackets and anchor points.

cable_tray.type: Cantilever brackets or threaded rod trapeze
cable_tray.notes: Standard for perforated/ladder tray up to 600mm wide. Wider tray may need closer spacing.

cable_ladder.notes: Heavy duty — check load capacity and spanning capability.

trunking.notes: Smaller trunking may be surface mounted with direct fixings.

conduit.notes: Closer spacing to prevent sagging between supports.

rectangular.type: Trapeze hangers with threaded rod, angle iron cradles
rectangular.notes: Standard for rectangular duct. May need closer spacing for large/heavy ducts.

circular.type: Ring hangers, band supports, clevis hangers
circular.notes: Circular duct is self-supporting over longer spans than rectangular.

flexible.notes: Flex duct must not sag — support at closer centres than rigid duct.

riser_supports.notes: Add 20% for vertical duct supports. Riser sections need angle brackets and thrust restraints.

general_rules: Support quantities are estimates — actual spacing depends on structural capacity of fixing points.
Threaded rod lengths depend on ceiling void depth — flag if not known.
Earthquake/seismic bracing: not standard UK but flag if spec mentions it.
Always check spec for specific support requirements — they override these defaults.
For heavy equipment (>50kg): engineer-designed supports required. Count as provisional item.

linear.reason: Ensures sufficient material on site. Pipe and cable come in standard lengths — rounding up accounts for this.

area.reason: Sheet materials cut from standard sizes — rounding up covers cutting waste.

number.reason: Cannot order fractional equipment. Always round up to next whole unit.

weight.reason: Structural steel ordered in standard sections — round up for cutting waste.

rounding.golden_rule: NEVER round DOWN. Always favour excess over shortage. It is cheaper to have surplus material on site than to delay the programme waiting for a shortfall order.

scale_bar.name: Scale Bar Method
scale_bar.description: Use the printed scale bar on the drawing to calibrate measurements.
scale_bar.accuracy: Highest — scale bar scales with the print, compensating for print size changes.
scale_bar.when_to_use: PREFERRED method. Use whenever a scale bar is present on the drawing.
scale_bar.how: Measure the scale bar, calculate pixels-per-metre or mm-per-metre ratio, apply to all measurements on that sheet.

stated_scale.name: Stated Scale Method
stated_scale.description: Use the scale ratio stated in the title block (e.g. 1:100).
stated_scale.accuracy: Good — but assumes the drawing was printed at the stated paper size (e.g. A1).
stated_scale.when_to_use: When no scale bar is present but title block states a scale and paper size.
stated_scale.caveat: If the drawing was printed on different paper (e.g. A1 drawing printed at A3), the stated scale is WRONG. Flag this risk.
stated_scale.how: At 1:100 on A1 paper, 1mm on paper = 100mm (0.1m) in reality.

coordinate.name: Coordinate / Grid Method
coordinate.description: Use building grid line spacings (known dimensions) to derive measurements.
coordinate.accuracy: Moderate — depends on grid accuracy and interpolation between grid lines.
coordinate.when_to_use: For complex routing where scale bar is absent, or to verify scale bar measurements.
coordinate.how: Identify two grid lines with known spacing (e.g. grids A-B = 7.5m). Measure the grid spacing on the drawing. Use ratio to calculate other distances.

annotation.name: Annotated Dimensions
annotation.description: Use dimensions explicitly annotated on the drawing.
annotation.accuracy: Highest — figured dimensions always override measured/scaled dimensions.
annotation.when_to_use: Wherever annotated dimensions are provided. These override all other methods.
annotation.rule: FIGURED DIMENSIONS ALWAYS OVERRIDE SCALED MEASUREMENTS. If a drawing says "15.0m" but you measure 14.2m from scale, the answer is 15.0m.

no_scale_protocol: If NO scale bar AND no stated scale AND no grid reference dimensions:
1. Flag immediately: "No measurement reference available — cannot extract linear quantities".
2. Extract point counts (nr items) only — these do not depend on scale.
3. Extract annotated dimensions only (sizes, ratings, specs from labels).
4. Set confidence to "Low" for any length estimates.
5. Recommend estimator obtains scaled drawings or site survey before finalising.
measurement.measurement_priority: Annotated dimensions > Scale bar > Stated scale > Grid method > Do not measure

FIRM.label: Firm Quantity
FIRM.description: Extracted from approved construction drawings. Reliable for ordering and pricing.
FIRM.action: Use directly for pricing and material ordering.

PRELIMINARY.label: Preliminary Quantity
PRELIMINARY.description: Extracted from non-final drawings. Routing and quantities may change.
PRELIMINARY.action: Use for budget estimates only. Add contingency. Do not order materials.

TENDER.label: Tender Quantity
TENDER.description: Extracted from tender issue drawings. May differ from construction issue.
TENDER.action: Use for tender pricing. Note that construction issue may change quantities.

ESTIMATED.label: Estimated Quantity
ESTIMATED.description: Quantity derived from estimation, not direct measurement. Low confidence.
ESTIMATED.action: Flag for estimator review. State basis of estimate.

FROM_SCHEDULE.label: Schedule Quantity
FROM_SCHEDULE.description: Quantity taken directly from a schedule rather than measured from drawing.
FROM_SCHEDULE.action: Cross-reference schedule quantity with drawing count where possible.
FROM_SCHEDULE.notes: Schedule quantities are authoritative when they exist.

quantity_status.rule: ALWAYS state quantity status in extraction output. Every line item must have a status classification.
quantity_status.contingency_note: Contingency percentages are recommendations — estimator may adjust based on project knowledge.
